description: Equation (1) defines the level-set function \( S_D(\tilde{x}, \tilde{y}, \tilde{z}) \) for the Schwarz \( D \) triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS). Here, \( \tilde{x}, \tilde{y}, \tilde{z} \) denote dimensionless spatial coordinates, and \( S_D \) is a trigonometric sum composed of products of sines and cosines of these coordinates. The equation \( S_D = 0 \) implicitly describes the Schwarz \( D \) surface, where the zero-level set corresponds to the continuous, periodic interface. This functional representation allows the TPMS geometry to be generated and analyzed mathematically, facilitating the identification of atomic positions in structures such as diamond gyroidal bicontinuous (DGB) networks.

Equation (2) defines the implicit surface function \( S_P(\tilde{x}, \tilde{y}, \tilde{z}) \) for the Schwarz \( P \) minimal surface, where \( \tilde{x}, \tilde{y}, \tilde{z} \) are dimensionless reduced coordinates. These coordinates are related to the Cartesian positions \( (x, y, z) \) within an orthogonal simulation box via the scaling \( \tilde{x} = x \cdot m\pi / l_x \), \( \tilde{y} = y \cdot n\pi / l_y \), and \( \tilde{z} = z \cdot p\pi / l_z \); here, \( m, n, p \) denote the number of fundamental unit cells along the \( X \), \( Y \), and \( Z \) lattice directions, respectively, and \( l_x, l_y, l_z \) are the corresponding box dimensions. The equation \( \cos 2\tilde{x} + \cos 2\tilde{y} + \cos 2\tilde{z} = 0 \) describes the locus of points that constitute the triply periodic minimal surface, with its zero-level isosurface representing the interface geometry. This trigonometric form captures the inherent symmetry and periodicity of the Schwarz \( P \) surface, which partitions space into two interpenetrating, non-intersecting channel networks.

The equation defines the reduced Cartesian coordinate \(\tilde{x}\) for an atom within a simulation box containing a periodic minimal surface structure. Here, \(x\) denotes the atom's original Cartesian coordinate along the \(X\)-axis, \(m\) is the number of fundamental unit cells of the surface structure replicated along the \(X\)-direction, and \(l_x\) is the length of the orthogonal simulation box along the same axis. The transformation scales the physical coordinate \(x\) by the factor \(m\pi / l_x\), effectively normalizing it with respect to the box dimension and the periodicity of the surface. This non-dimensionalization maps the atom's position into a reduced coordinate system that is convenient for describing periodic structures like the Schwarz \(D\) and \(P\) surfaces.

The equation defines the reduced coordinate \(\tilde{y}\) for an atom within a periodic structure. Here, \(y\) denotes the atom's actual Cartesian coordinate along the \(Y\)-axis, \(n\) represents the number of fundamental unit cells along this direction within the simulation box, and \(l_y\) is the physical length of the box along \(Y\). The transformation scales the physical coordinate by the factor \(n\pi / l_y\), effectively normalizing it by the box dimension and mapping it onto a dimensionless domain scaled by \(\pi\). This non-dimensionalization is part of a coordinate transformation applied to facilitate the mathematical description of atomic positions within a supercell composed of \(m \times n \times p\) fundamental units of a surface structure.

The equation defines the reduced coordinate \(\tilde{z}\) for an atom within a periodic structure. Here, \(z\) denotes the atom's original Cartesian coordinate along the \(Z\)-axis, \(p\) represents the number of fundamental cell units along the (001) lattice direction, and \(l_y\) is the box length along the \(Y\)-axis. The transformation scales the physical coordinate \(z\) by the dimensionless factor \(p\pi / l_y\), thereby converting it into a reduced, dimensionless coordinate \(\tilde{z}\) that is normalized by the system's periodicity. This scaling is part of a consistent set of transformations applied to all Cartesian coordinates to facilitate the analysis of atomic positions within the orthogonal simulation box containing \(m \times n \times p\) fundamental units of the Schwarz crystal structure.

Equation (3) expresses the capillary pressure, \( P \), acting on a grain boundary (GB) according to the Young-Laplace law for a curved interface. In this relationship, \( \gamma_{GB} \) denotes the grain boundary energy per unit area, and \( R \) represents the mean radius of curvature of the boundary. The equation describes a fundamental inverse proportionality: the pressure is directly proportional to twice the boundary energy and inversely proportional to the radius of curvature. Consequently, for a boundary with a large radius of curvature (approaching a flat configuration), the capillary pressure becomes minimal, as indicated in the provided context.

Equation (4) quantifies the additional pressure, \(S\), that arises at a grain boundary (GB) due to an applied shear stress, \(\tau\), acting on neighboring crystalline lattices. Here, \(E_A\) and \(E_B\) denote the Young’s moduli of the adjacent grains \(A\) and \(B\), respectively. The relationship shows that \(S\) is proportional to the square of the applied stress and depends on the difference in the mechanical compliance (\(1/E\)) of the two grains. Since the intrinsic anisotropy of crystals generally ensures \(E_A \neq E_B\), the term in parentheses remains finite, indicating that a persistent driving force for GB motion can be generated under stress, even in the absence of other chemical or thermal gradients.

Equation (5) defines the condition for the thermal instability of a supercrystal (SC) under an applied pressure. Here, \( T_S \) denotes the temperature at which this instability occurs, and \( p(T_S) \) represents the total pressure acting on the system at that temperature. This total pressure is expressed as the sum of two temperature-dependent components: \( P(T_S) \), which is the externally applied mechanical pressure, and \( S(T_S) \), which accounts for additional stress contributions, such as those arising from interfacial or coherency stresses within the microstructure. The equation states that when this combined pressure \( p(T_S) \) equals a critical value \( P_c \)—the threshold pressure required for grain boundary migration—the supercrystal becomes thermally unstable. Thus, the equation provides a criterion for determining the instability temperature \( T_S \) by balancing the net internal pressure against the critical migration pressure.

Equation (6) describes the asymptotic behavior of the time-averaged entropy, \(\langle S(T) \rangle\), of the grain boundary (GB) system as the temperature approaches the GB melting point, \(T_m^{\mathrm{GB}}\). Here, the angle brackets \(\langle \cdot \rangle\) denote a statistical or temporal average, and \(S(T)\) represents the configurational entropy at temperature \(T\). The relation \(\langle S(T) \rangle \rightarrow 0\) signifies that, under a minimal pressure condition, the averaged entropy vanishes as \(T \leq T_m^{\mathrm{GB}}\), irrespective of thermal fluctuations and critical pressure \(P_c\). This limit indicates the suppression of accessible microstates and the onset of a highly ordered, low-entropy state at the GB melting transition.

Equation (7) expresses the condition of minimal pressure sustained by a grain boundary (GB) up to its melting point. Here, \( p(T) \) denotes the total effective pressure acting on the GB, which is decomposed into a static pressure component \( P(T) \) and the thermal average of a stress fluctuation term \( \langle S(T) \rangle \). The relation states that the sum of these two contributions tends to zero, \( p(T) \rightarrow 0 \), for temperatures \( T \leq T_m^{\mathrm{GB}} \). This indicates that the internal static pressure is precisely balanced by the thermally averaged stress fluctuations, establishing a near-zero net pressure condition that is maintained irrespective of the critical pressure \( P_c \) and other thermal effects until the GB melts.

The equation \( E_A = E_B \) expresses a condition of mechanical equilibrium under zero applied pressure. Here, \( E_A \) and \( E_B \) denote the elastic energies of two distinct structural domains or phases, labeled A and B, respectively. The equality signifies that these energies are balanced, implying no net driving force for transformation or migration between the domains. In the context of grain boundary systems, this condition is associated with a minimal mean driving force for boundary motion, particularly under the constraints of coherent twin boundaries.

The equation defines a critical size threshold for a structural feature within the material system. Here, \( D_S \) denotes the aperture size of a D-shaped structural component (D-SC). The relationship \( D_S = 3 \, \mathrm{nm} \) specifies the minimum size limit above which grain boundary (GB) migration is completely suppressed, thereby conferring maximum thermal stability to the structures labeled S0 and S1 at temperatures approaching the material's melting point.
